title: How to Rent a Boat in Greece: A Practical Guide for First-Time Visitors
topic: How to rent a boat in Greece
html:
Few countries reward a day on the water quite like Greece. With thousands of islands, sheltered bays and warm clear seas, a boat turns a good holiday into a memorable one. Whether you picture a lazy morning swim off a quiet cove or a longer hop between islands, renting a boat here is more straightforward than most first-time visitors assume. This guide explains how it works, what to expect, and how to choose well.

Decide where and when you want to sail

Greece is not one single sailing region but many. The Cyclades, including Mykonos, Paros and Naxos, are famous for crystal water and the strong summer wind known as the Meltemi. The Ionian islands such as Corfu, Lefkada and Kefalonia are calmer and greener, which makes them friendlier for beginners. The Saronic Gulf near Athens is ideal for short trips because the islands sit close together. The season runs roughly from late April to October, with July and August the busiest and hottest months. If you can travel in May, June or September you will find better availability and gentler prices when you rent a boat for the week.

Choose the right type of boat

The boat you pick shapes the whole day, so start from the experience you want rather than the price tag.

  • Motorboats and RIBs: fast and flexible, perfect for reaching several beaches in a single day. Small engines can often be driven without a licence.
  • Sailing yachts: the classic choice for island hopping over several days, quieter and more economical on fuel.
  • Catamarans: stable, spacious and shaded, well suited to families or groups who value comfort.
  • Motor yachts: a touch of luxury with crew, ideal if you want to relax and let someone else handle the navigation.

You can compare these side by side and check real availability before you commit. It helps to browse boats in your chosen region first, then narrow down by size, budget and the number of guests on board.

Skippered or bareboat

One of the first questions any operator will ask is whether you want a skipper. A skippered charter comes with a local captain who knows the winds, the anchorages and the best swim stops, and requires no qualifications from you. A bareboat charter lets you take the helm yourself, but for larger sailing yachts Greek regulations require a recognised licence and often proof of sailing experience for a second crew member. If you are new to boating, hiring a skipper for at least the first day is money well spent. Many people who plan a longer trip use a yacht charter with a skipper and learn enough to feel confident by the end of the week.

What is usually included, and what is not

Rates vary between operators, so read each listing carefully. As a rough guide:

  • Insurance, safety equipment and basic mooring gear are normally included in the price.
  • Fuel is often charged separately, paid on return based on what you use.
  • A refundable security deposit is common, especially for bareboat hires.
  • Extras such as a stand-up paddleboard, snorkelling kit or a skipper for the day may cost more.

Always confirm the cancellation policy and the fuel arrangement in writing before you pay. A clear listing on the platform you hire a boat through will spell these details out so there are no surprises at the dock.

Book early and travel prepared

Summer demand in Greece is high, and the best boats in popular harbours sell out weeks ahead. Reserve early, bring any licence and identification you may need, and pack sun protection, soft-soled shoes and a light layer for the breeze. Check the forecast on the morning of your trip, since the Meltemi can pick up quickly in the Aegean. Agree a meeting time and place with the owner, and ask for a short handover so you know the basics before you cast off.

A good day on the water starts with a clear plan: know your route, your timing and your weather before you leave the quay.

Own a boat in Greece

If you keep a boat that sits idle for much of the season, you can earn from it during the busy months. It takes only a few minutes to list your boat and reach travellers searching for exactly the kind of trip your vessel offers. From a single RIB to a crewed yacht, putting it in front of a worldwide audience is the simplest way to turn quiet weeks into income.
